title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text: In this chapter, you will learn the fundamental principles and key components of ggplot2. You will also gain practical experience in using these components to create statistical graphics based on the Layered Grammar of Graphics. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to apply the main graphical elements in ggplot2 to produce statistical charts that are both visually clear and functionally effective.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
1.3 Introducing ggplot





ggplot2 is an R package used to create statistical graphics and data visualisations. It is based on the Grammar of Graphics, which means a chart is built layer by layer using data, aesthetics, geometric objects, and visual settings.
In simple terms, ggplot2 helps us turn data into clear charts such as bar charts, scatter plots, line graphs, boxplots, and histograms. It is widely used because it produces professional-looking visualisations and gives users strong control over how the chart is displayed.
For more detail, visit ggplot2 link.
_________________________________________________________________________________________________________
Here are 2 ggplot2 cheatsheets which provide a quick reference for building and customising charts in R. It summarises the main components of ggplot2, such as aesthetics, geometries, scales, themes, labels, and faceting.
It helps users quickly choose the right chart type, understand the required syntax, and adjust visual elements without memorising every function.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
1.3.1 Plotting a simple bar chart
The bar chart below shows the number of students in each race category. Chinese students form the largest group, followed by Malay students. Indian and Others have much smaller counts.
This code also changes the bar chart colour by adding colour settings inside geom_bar().
geom_bar(fill = "#2A6F6F", color = "#1F1F1F", linewidth = 0.35)
fill = "#2A6F6F" changes the inside colour of the bars to deep teal. color = "#1F1F1F" changes the outline colour of the bars to dark grey/black. linewidth = 0.35 controls the thickness of the bar outline.
So, instead of using the default grey bars, the chart now uses a cleaner and more customised colour style.

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#2A6F6F", color = "#1F1F1F", linewidth = 0.35) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Students by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Number of Students"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#F7F5EF", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#F7F5EF", color = NA),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#D8D2C4"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "#E8E2D6")
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
1.3.2 R Graphics VS ggplot
First, let us compare how R Graphics and ggplot2 plot a simple histogram.

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(
  exam_data$MATHS,
  col = "#2A6F6F",
  border = "#1F1F1F"
)











ggplot(data = exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F", 
                 fill = "#2A6F6F") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")











Although the code chunk is relatively simple when using R Graphics, this raises an important question: why is ggplot2 still recommended?
As pointed out by Hadley Wickham
“The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.”
Another reason why ggplot2 is preferred is that it usually produces more visually appealing charts. Compared with Base R Graphics, ggplot2 has a cleaner default layout, better spacing, and more consistent visual design. This makes the chart easier to read and more suitable for reports, presentations, and websites.

plot(exam_data$MATHS, exam_data$SCIENCE,
     col = as.numeric(as.factor(exam_data$RACE)),
     pch = as.numeric(as.factor(exam_data$GENDER)),
     main = "Maths Scores vs Science Scores",
     xlab = "Maths Scores",
     ylab = "Science Scores")









ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = SCIENCE,
           color = RACE,
           shape = RACE)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Maths Scores vs Science Scores",
    x = "Maths Scores",
    y = "Science Scores",
    color = "Race",
    shape = "Race"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
1.4 Grammar of Graphics
Before getting started with ggplot2, it is important to understand the principles of the Grammar of Graphics.
The Grammar of Graphics is a general framework for data visualisation. It breaks a graph down into meaningful components, such as data, aesthetics, scales, and layers. This concept was introduced by Leland Wilkinson in The Grammar of Graphics (1999). The Grammar of Graphics helps answer an important question:
What is a statistical graphic?
In short, the Grammar of Graphics defines how mathematical and visual elements can be structured to create a meaningful graph.
There are two key principles in the Grammar of Graphics:
1. A graphic is built from distinct layers of grammatical elements. 2. A meaningful plot is created through aesthetic mapping.
A good grammar of graphics helps us understand how complex graphics are constructed. It can also reveal connections between different types of charts that may not seem related at first. More importantly, it provides a foundation for understanding a wide range of visualisations and helps us judge whether a graph is properly structured. However, even if a graph is grammatically correct, it may still be meaningless or misleading if the design does not support a clear analytical purpose.
ggplot2 is an implementation of Leland Wilkinson’s Grammar of Graphics. Figure below shows the seven grammars of ggplot2.



Reference: Hadley Wickham (2010) “A layered grammar of graphics.” Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics, vol. 19, no. 1, pp. 3–28.


A short description of each building block are as follows:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics, statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate systems define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
1.5 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2
This section focuses on how data, aesthetics, geometries, and other elements work together to create meaningful visualisations.

1.5.1 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: data
Let us call the ggplot() function using the code chunk on the right.

ggplot(data=exam_data)














NoteNote




A blank canvas appears.
ggplot() initializes a ggplot object.
The data argument defines the dataset to be used for plotting.
If the dataset is not already a data.frame, it will be converted to one by fortify().





1.5.2 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: Aesthetic mappings
Aesthetic mappings use variables from the dataset to control visual properties of a plot, such as position, colour, size, shape, and transparency. These visual properties help represent information from the data.
In ggplot2, aesthetic mappings are defined inside the aes() function. Later in this lesson, we will see that each geom layer can also have its own aesthetic mapping.
The code chunk below adds an aesthetic element to the plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS))














NoteNote




ggplot includes the x-axis and the axis’s label.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
section: 1.6 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: geom
text:
1.6 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: geom
Geometric objects are the actual marks we put on a plot. Examples include:

geom_point for drawing individual points (e.g., a scatter plot)
geom_line for drawing lines (e.g., for a line charts)
geom_smooth for drawing smoothed lines (e.g., for simple trends or approximations)
geom_bar for drawing bars (e.g., for bar charts)
geom_histogram for drawing binned values (e.g. a histogram)
geom_polygon for drawing arbitrary shapes
geom_map for drawing polygons in the shape of a map! (You can access the data to use for these maps by using the map_data() function).




Image retrieved from:https://r4va.netlify.app/chap01



A plot must have at least one geom; there is no upper limit. You can add a geom to a plot using the + operator.
For complete list, please refer to here.


1.6.1 Geometric Objects: geom_dotplot
In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.
In the code chunk below, geom_dotplot() of ggplot2 is used to plot a dot plot.

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5,
               fill = "#2A6F6F",
               color = "#1F1F1F")

Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.















WarningWarning



The y scale is not very useful. In fact, it can be very misleading.








NoteNote



The code chunk below performs the following two steps:

scale_y_continuous() is used to turn off the y-axis.
The binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.




ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5,
               fill = "#2A6F6F",
               color = "#1F1F1F")

Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.











1.6.2 Geometric Objects: geom_histogram()
In the code chunk below, geom_histogram() is used to create a simple histogram by using values in MATHS field of exam_data.

ggplot(data = exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 linewidth = 0.35)

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.















NoteNote



Note that the default number of bins is 30.




1.6.3 Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
In the code chunk below,

bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  










1.6.4 Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
The code chunk below changes the interior colour of the histogram (i.e. fill) by using sub-group of aesthetic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")














NoteNote



This approach can be used to control the colour, fill, and alpha of the geometry.




1.6.5 Geometric Objects: geom-density()
geom_density() computes and plots a kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of a histogram.
It is useful for visualising continuous data when the distribution is expected to be relatively smooth.
The code below shows the distribution of Maths scores using a kernel density estimate plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()  








The code chunk below plots two kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()










1.6.6 Geometric Objects: geom_boxplot
geom_boxplot() is used to display the distribution of a continuous variable.
It visualises key summary statistics, including the median, the lower and upper hinges, the whiskers, and any outliers.
The code chunk below creates boxplots using geom_boxplot().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()         









Notches are used in box plots to help visually assess whether the medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.
The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)










1.6.7 Geometric Objects: geom_violin
geom_violin() is used to create violin plots, which help compare multiple data distributions.
Unlike ordinary density curves, violin plots place distributions side by side, making them easier to compare without visual overlap.
The code below shows the distribution of Maths scores by gender using a violin plot.

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "#2A6F6F",
              color = "#1F1F1F",
              linewidth = 0.35)










1.6.8 Geometric Objects: geom_point()
geom_point() is useful when it comes to creating scatterplot.
The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils by using geom_point().

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(shape = 21,
             fill = "#2A6F6F",
             color = "#1F1F1F")










1.6.9 Combining geom_boxplot( ) and geom_point( )
The code chunk below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#2A6F6F",
               color = "#1F1F1F",
               linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_point(position = "jitter",
             size = 0.5,
             color = "#1F1F1F")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
section: 1.7 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: stat
text:
1.7 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: stat
Statistical functions transform data into summary information before plotting. For example, they can calculate the frequency of values in a variable for a bar chart, the mean value, or confidence limits.
There are two main ways to use these functions in ggplot2. The first way is to use a stat_() function and change its default geom. The second way is to use a geom_() function and change its default stat.

1.7.1 Working with stat()
The boxplots below are incomplete because the positions of the means were not shown.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()










1.7.3 Working with stat - the stat_summary() method
The code chunk below adds mean values by using stat_summary() function and overriding the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)  










1.7.4 Working with stat - the geom() method
The code chunk below adding mean values by using geom_() function and overriding the default stat.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)  










1.7.4 Adding a best fit curve on a scatterplot?
The scatterplot below shows the relationship of Maths and English grades of pupils. The interpretability of this graph can be improved by adding a best fit curve.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()








In the code chunk below, geom_smooth() is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.

ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(color = "#1F1F1F") +
  geom_smooth(linewidth = 0.5,
              color = "#2A6F6F",
              fill = "#2A6F6F")

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'















NoteNote



The default method used is loess.


#Before using stat_poly_line() and stat_poly_eq(), the ggpmisc package must be installed and loaded. The package only needs to be installed once, but it must be loaded with library() each time a new R session starts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
section: 1.8 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: Facets
text:
1.8 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: Facets
Faceting creates small multiple plots, where each plot shows a different subset of the data. It is another way to display additional categorical variables without adding more aesthetics such as colour or shape.
ggplot2 supports two main faceting functions: facet_grid() and facet_wrap().

1.8.1 Working with facet_wrap()
facet_wrap wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet-wrap().

ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS)










1.8.2 facet_grid() function
facet_grid() creates a matrix of panels based on row and column faceting variables. It is most useful when the dataset contains two categorical variables and all combinations of these variables are available.
The code chunk below creates a trellis plot using facet_grid().

ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
section: 1.9 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: Coordinates
text:
1.9 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: Coordinates
The Coordinates functions map the position of objects onto the plane of the plot. There are a number of different possible coordinate systems to use, they are:
-   [`coord_cartesian()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_cartesian.html): the default cartesian coordinate systems, where you specify x and y values (e.g. allows you to zoom in or out).
-   [`coord_flip()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_flip.html): a cartesian system with the x and y flipped.
-   [`coord_fixed()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_fixed.html): a cartesian system with a "fixed" aspect ratio (e.g. 1.78 for a "widescreen" plot).
-   [`coord_quickmap()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_map.html): a coordinate system that approximates a good aspect ratio for maps.

1.9.1 Working with Coordinate
By the default, the bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.
The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#2A6F6F",
           color = "#1F1F1F",
           linewidth = 0.35) +
  coord_flip()








1.9.2 Changing the y- and x-axis range
The scatterplot on the right is slightly misleading because the y-aixs and x-axis range are not equal.

ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = "#2A6F6F")

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.

ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(color = "#1F1F1F") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, 
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = "#2A6F6F",
              fill = "#2A6F6F") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100))

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Hands-on Exercise 1:A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
section: 1.10 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: themes
text:
1.10 Essential Grammatical Elements in ggplot2: themes
Themes control elements of the graph not related to the data. For example:

background colour
size of fonts
gridlines
colour of labels

Built-in themes include: - theme_gray() (default) - theme_bw() - theme_classic()
A list of theme can be found at this link. Each theme element can be conceived of as either a line (e.g. x-axis), a rectangle (e.g. graph background), or text (e.g. axis title).

1.10.1 Working with theme
The code chunk below plot a horizontal bar chart using theme_gray().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_classic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_minimal().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this hands-on exercise, we will learn how to create interactive data visualisations in R. The focus is on using ggiraph and plotly to move beyond static charts and allow readers to explore the data directly.
By the end of this exercise, we will be able to:

create interactive ggplot graphics with ggiraph,
add tooltip, hover, and click effects,
create interactive scatter plots with plotly,
convert ggplot charts into interactive charts,
build coordinated views across multiple charts and tables.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this hands-on exercise, we will learn how to create interactive data visualisations in R. The focus is on using ggiraph and plotly to move beyond static charts and allow readers to explore the data directly.
By the end of this exercise, we will be able to:

create interactive ggplot graphics with ggiraph,
add tooltip, hover, and click effects,
create interactive scatter plots with plotly,
convert ggplot charts into interactive charts,
build coordinated views across multiple charts and tables.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.2 Getting Started
Before creating the interactive charts, the required R packages need to be loaded. These packages support interactive graphics, data manipulation, interactive tables, and linked visualisation.

ggiraph is used to make ggplot graphics interactive.
plotly is used to create interactive web-based statistical charts.
DT is used to display interactive data tables.
tidyverse is used for data import, wrangling, and ggplot2 graphics.
patchwork is used to combine several ggplot2 charts into one layout.
crosstalk is used for linked interactions between widgets.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.3 Importing Data
The dataset used in this exercise is Exam_data.csv. It contains student-level examination scores and related student information. The data is imported with read_csv() and saved as a tibble called exam_data.

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")







TipSmall note



The file Exam_data.csv must be placed in the same folder as Hands-on_Ex03.qmd. If the file is stored somewhere else, the path inside read_csv() must be changed.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.4 Interactive Data Visualisation - ggiraph methods
ggiraph is an extension of ggplot2 that turns static ggplot charts into interactive SVG graphics. This is useful when we want readers to inspect individual data points instead of only looking at the overall pattern.
The main interactive features are:

tooltip: shows information when the mouse hovers over a data point.
onclick: runs a JavaScript action when an element is clicked.
data_id: links graphic elements to an identifier, which is useful for hover and coordinated highlighting.


3.4.1 Tooltip effect with tooltip aesthetic
This first example creates an interactive dot plot of Maths scores. Each dot represents a student, and the tooltip shows the student ID when the reader hovers over the dot.

ViewCode











p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Interactive Dot Plot of Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)




The chart is created in two steps. First, geom_dotplot_interactive() builds the interactive ggplot object. Then, girafe() converts the ggplot object into an interactive visual that can be displayed on an HTML page.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.5 Interactivity
The simplest form of interactivity is a tooltip. When the reader hovers over a dot, the student ID appears. This makes the chart more useful because individual observations can be inspected without adding too many permanent labels to the plot.

3.5.1 Displaying multiple information on tooltip
A tooltip can also show more than one piece of information. In this example, the tooltip combines student ID and class into one text field.

ViewCode











exam_data <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    tooltip_basic = paste0(
      "Student ID: ", ID,
      "\nClass: ", CLASS
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_basic),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Maths Scores with Student and Class Tooltip",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)




Here, a new column called tooltip_basic is created. This column stores the text that will appear in the tooltip. This is cleaner than writing long tooltip logic directly inside aes().

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.6 Interactivity
This section expands the use of ggiraph by adding customised tooltip styles, statistical summaries, hover effects, click actions, and coordinated multiple views.

3.6.1 Customising Tooltip style
The tooltip does not have to use the default style. We can use CSS to control its background colour, text colour, font weight, padding, and shape.

ViewCode











tooltip_css <- paste0(
  "background-color:", my_dark, ";",
  "color:white;",
  "padding:6px 9px;",
  "border-radius:6px;",
  "font-weight:600;",
  "font-size:12px;"
)

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Tooltip Styling with ggiraph",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)










TipWhat can we learn?



opts_tooltip() controls how the tooltip looks. This is useful when the chart needs to match a website or report colour style. Here, the tooltip uses a dark background with white text to match the muted teal visual design.




3.6.2 Displaying statistics on tooltip
Tooltips can also show calculated statistics. In this example, the chart summarises Maths scores by race and displays the mean with the standard error range in the tooltip.

ViewCode











tooltip_stat <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = 0.01) {
  mean_value <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem_value <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean Maths score:", mean_value, "+/-", sem_value)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(
      y = MATHS,
      tooltip = after_stat(tooltip_stat(y, ymax))
    ),
    fun.data = "mean_se",
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    linewidth = 0.35,
    color = my_dark
  ) +
  labs(title = "Mean Maths Scores by Race",
       subtitle = "Tooltip shows mean and standard error",
       x = "Race",
       y = "Mean Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)




This example is more advanced because the tooltip is not taken directly from the raw dataset. Instead, it is built from summary statistics calculated by stat_summary().


3.6.3 Hover effect with data_id aesthetic
The data_id aesthetic is used to connect visual elements to an identifier. When the mouse moves over one element, related elements with the same data_id can be highlighted.

ViewCode











p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Hover Highlight by Class",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)




When the reader hovers over one dot, dots from the same class are highlighted. This makes class-level grouping easier to notice.


3.6.4 Styling hover effect
The hover effect can be styled with CSS. Here, the selected dots become stronger, while the non-selected dots become faded.

ViewCode











p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Custom Hover Style",
       subtitle = "Highlighted points stay strong while other points fade",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)










TipWhat can we learn?



opts_hover() controls the selected item, while opts_hover_inv() controls the non-selected items. This is useful because interactivity should guide attention, not just decorate the chart.




3.6.5 Combining tooltip and hover effect
Tooltip and hover effects can be used together. The tooltip gives detailed information, while the hover effect highlights related points.

ViewCode











p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(
      tooltip = CLASS,
      data_id = CLASS
    ),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Tooltip and Hover Effect Combined",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css),
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)




In this chart, the tooltip shows the class, and the hover effect highlights students from the same class. This gives both detail and pattern recognition in the same visual.


3.6.6 Click effect with onclick
The onclick aesthetic allows an action to happen when the reader clicks on a chart element. In web-based visualisation, this can be used to open a link or trigger another action.






WarningWarning



Click actions must be stored as valid JavaScript strings. If the JavaScript syntax is wrong, the chart may still render, but the click action will not work correctly.



ViewCode











exam_data <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    onclick = sprintf(
      "window.open('%s')",
      "https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school"
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(onclick = onclick,
        tooltip = paste0("Click to open school finder\nStudent ID: ", ID)),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Click Interaction with onclick",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)




This example shows how interactive charts can connect the visual object to an external web action. The chart is no longer just for viewing; it can also become a navigation tool.


3.6.7 Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
Coordinated multiple views allow different charts to respond to the same interaction. In this example, Maths and English dot plots are placed side by side. Because both charts use the same student ID as data_id, hovering over a student in one chart highlights the same student in the other chart.

ViewCode











p1 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = ID,
        tooltip = paste0("Student ID: ", ID, "\nMaths: ", MATHS)),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p2 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = ID,
        tooltip = paste0("Student ID: ", ID, "\nEnglish: ", ENGLISH)),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "English Scores",
       x = "English score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  code = print(p1 + p2),
  width_svg = 9,
  height_svg = 4,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css),
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)










TipWhat can we learn?



The key argument is data_id = ID. Because the same student ID is used in both plots, ggiraph can recognise that the two points refer to the same observation.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.7 Interactive Data Visualisation - plotly methods
plotly creates interactive web graphics in R. It can be used directly with plot_ly(), or it can convert existing ggplot2 charts into interactive charts with ggplotly().
There are two common ways to use plotly:

plot_ly() creates an interactive chart directly.
ggplotly() converts a ggplot chart into an interactive plotly chart.


3.7.1 Creating an interactive scatter plot: plot_ly() method
This example creates a basic interactive scatter plot using plot_ly(). The x-axis shows Maths scores, while the y-axis shows English scores.

ViewCode











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  y = ~ENGLISH,
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers",
  marker = list(
    color = my_teal,
    size = 8,
    opacity = 0.75,
    line = list(color = my_dark, width = 0.6)
  ),
  text = ~paste(
    "Student ID:", ID,
    "<br>Class:", CLASS,
    "<br>Maths:", MATHS,
    "<br>English:", ENGLISH
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "English Scores versus Maths Scores",
    xaxis = list(title = "Maths score", range = c(0, 100)),
    yaxis = list(title = "English score", range = c(0, 100))
  )




This chart is interactive immediately. The reader can hover, zoom, pan, and inspect exact values without extra ggplot layers.


3.7.2 Working with visual variable: plot_ly() method
A visual variable can be mapped to a data field. Here, RACE is mapped to colour, allowing the reader to compare score patterns across groups.

ViewCode











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~ENGLISH,
  y = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  colors = c(my_teal, my_soft_teal, "#D9B08C", "#5B6770", "#A0A0A0"),
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers",
  marker = list(
    size = 8,
    opacity = 0.75,
    line = list(color = my_dark, width = 0.4)
  ),
  text = ~paste(
    "Student ID:", ID,
    "<br>Race:", RACE,
    "<br>English:", ENGLISH,
    "<br>Maths:", MATHS
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Maths and English Scores by Race",
    xaxis = list(title = "English score", range = c(0, 100)),
    yaxis = list(title = "Maths score", range = c(0, 100))
  )










TipInteraction to try



Click one race category in the legend. Plotly will hide or show that group, making it easier to compare one group at a time.




3.7.3 Creating an interactive scatter plot: ggplotly() method
If a chart has already been created with ggplot2, ggplotly() can convert it into an interactive chart. This is useful when we want to keep ggplot2 syntax but still add web interactivity.

ViewCode











p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH,
                text = paste0(
                  "Student ID: ", ID,
                  "\nClass: ", CLASS,
                  "\nMaths: ", MATHS,
                  "\nEnglish: ", ENGLISH
                ))) +
  geom_point(color = my_teal,
             size = 2,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = my_dark,
              se = TRUE) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Interactive ggplotly Scatter Plot",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "English score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")




Only one extra function, ggplotly(), is needed to turn the ggplot object into an interactive plotly chart.


3.7.4 Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly
Plotly can also create coordinated views. The idea is to build two related charts and link them through shared data. When a point is selected in one chart, the related point can be highlighted in another chart.

ViewCode











d <- highlight_key(exam_data, ~ID)

p1 <- ggplot(data = d,
             aes(x = MATHS,
                 y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(color = my_teal,
             size = 1.8,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "English versus Maths",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "English score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p2 <- ggplot(data = d,
             aes(x = MATHS,
                 y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(color = my_soft_teal,
             size = 1.8,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Science versus Maths",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "Science score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

subplot(
  ggplotly(p1),
  ggplotly(p2),
  nrows = 1,
  titleX = TRUE,
  titleY = TRUE
) %>%
  highlight(
    on = "plotly_selected",
    off = "plotly_deselect",
    persistent = TRUE
  )










TipWhat can we learn?



highlight_key() creates shared data. This shared data allows the two plotly charts to recognise that selected points refer to the same student.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.8 Interactive Data Visualisation - crosstalk methods
crosstalk allows different HTML widgets to communicate with one another. This is useful when we want a chart and a table to respond to the same selection.

3.8.1 Interactive Data Table: DT package
The DT package displays an R data frame as an interactive table. Readers can search, sort, and browse the data directly on the web page.

ViewCode











DT::datatable(
  exam_data,
  class = "compact stripe hover",
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(
    pageLength = 8,
    scrollX = TRUE
  )
)




The table gives readers a direct way to inspect the dataset. This is useful because interactive charts show patterns, while tables allow checking exact records.


3.8.2 Linked brushing: crosstalk method
Linked brushing connects a chart and a table. Selecting data points in the chart can highlight or filter the corresponding records in the table.

ViewCode





















d <- highlight_key(exam_data, ~ID)

p <- ggplot(d,
            aes(ENGLISH,
                MATHS)) +
  geom_point(color = my_teal,
             size = 1.8,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Linked Brushing between Chart and Table",
       x = "English score",
       y = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

gg <- highlight(
  ggplotly(p),
  on = "plotly_selected",
  off = "plotly_deselect",
  persistent = TRUE
)

crosstalk::bscols(
  widths = c(5, 7),
  gg,
  DT::datatable(
    d,
    class = "compact stripe hover",
    rownames = FALSE,
    options = list(
      pageLength = 8,
      scrollX = TRUE
    )
  )
)










WarningWarning



bscols() may load Bootstrap styling into the page. This is normal, but it can slightly affect the visual appearance of surrounding page elements.








TipWhat can we learn?



The chart and table are linked through the same shared data object. This makes it possible to move from visual exploration to exact record checking without leaving the page.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.9 Reference

3.9.1 ggiraph
ggiraph is useful when a ggplot chart needs lightweight interaction, especially tooltip, hover, and click effects. It works well when the goal is to keep the grammar of ggplot2 but make the output more exploratory.
Useful topics to review:

interactive geoms such as geom_point_interactive() and geom_dotplot_interactive(),
tooltip styling with opts_tooltip(),
hover styling with opts_hover() and opts_hover_inv(),
linking observations with data_id.



3.9.2 plotly for R
plotly is useful for creating interactive statistical graphics directly, or for converting ggplot2 charts into interactive web charts.
Useful topics to review:

plot_ly() for direct plotly charts,
ggplotly() for converting ggplot charts,
subplot() for placing multiple plotly charts together,
highlight_key() and highlight() for coordinated interactions.







TipFinal takeaway



ggiraph is closer to ggplot2 and is useful for controlled tooltip and hover design. plotly is stronger for zooming, selecting, and building interactive web charts quickly. DT and crosstalk are useful when the reader needs to connect charts with exact data records.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
This chapter introduces several ggplot2 extensions that enhance the clarity and visual quality of statistical graphics. By the end of this exercise, you will be able to:

Manage annotation placement on graphs using the ggrepel package,
Design publication-ready figures with the ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages,
Combine multiple ggplot2 graphs into a single composite figure using the patchwork package.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
This chapter introduces several ggplot2 extensions that enhance the clarity and visual quality of statistical graphics. By the end of this exercise, you will be able to:

Manage annotation placement on graphs using the ggrepel package,
Design publication-ready figures with the ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages,

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
2.2 Getting started

2.2.1 Installing and loading the required libraries
In this exercise, beside tidyverse, four R packages will be used. They are:

ggrepel: an R package provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text labels.
ggthemes: an R package provides some extra themes, geoms, and scales for ‘ggplot2’.
hrbrthemes: an R package provides typography-centric themes and theme components for ggplot2.
patchwork: an R package for preparing composite figure created using ggplot2.

The code chunk below checks whether the required packages are already installed and loads them into your working R environment.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse) 



2.2.2 Importing data
For the purpose of this exercise, a data file called Exam_data will be used. It consists of year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school. It is in csv file format.
The code chunk below imports exam_data.csv into R environment by using read_csv() function of readr package. readr is one of the tidyverse package.

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

Rows: 322 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE
dbl (3): ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.


The exam_data tibble contains seven attributes in total — four categorical and three continuous.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.



2.2.3 Understanding the Data
Before diving into the visualisations, it is good practice to get a quick overview of the dataset.

StructureMathsEnglishScience



glimpse(exam_data)

Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…





ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "steelblue",
                 color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths Score",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()











ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "salmon",
                 color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of English Scores",
       x = "English Score",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()











ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "seagreen",
                 color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Science Scores",
       x = "Science Score",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()











This keeps it short and purposeful — glimpse() gives a structural overview, and the histogram gives an immediate sense of the score distribution before any advanced plotting begins. You can swap MATHS for ENGLISH or SCIENCE to explore the other subjects too.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
2.3 Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel
One problem with this graph is that there are too many student labels crowded together, making the plot look messy and difficult to read. Instead of helping the viewer identify the data points, the annotations overlap and hide part of the pattern in the graph.

The plotThe code




`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'












ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(color = "#1F1F1F") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, 
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = "#2A6F6F") +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5,
             fill = "#2A6F6F",
             color = "white") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")




ggrepel is an extension package for ggplot2 that helps keep text labels from overlapping by automatically pushing them apart, as shown in the examples on the right.
We simply replace geom_text() by geom_text_repel() and geom_label() by geom_label_repel.

The plotThe code




`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'












ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, 
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = "#2A6F6F") +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
2.4 Beyond ggplot2 Themes
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes, they are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().

The plotThe code



ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")











ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")




Refer to this link to learn more about ggplot2 Themes

2.4.1 Working with ggtheme package
ggthemes is an extension package for ggplot2 that offers ready-made chart styles inspired by sources such as Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, FiveThirtyEight, The Economist, Stata, Excel, and The Wall Street Journal.
The example below, The Tufte theme is used.

The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_tufte()




It also provides some extra geoms and scales for ‘ggplot2’. Consult this vignette to learn more.


2.4.2 Working with hrbthemes package
hrbrthemes package provides a base theme that focuses on typographic elements, including where various labels are placed as well as the fonts that are used.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 linewidth = 0.35,
                 alpha = 0.88) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores",
       subtitle = "Most students scored between the mid-range and upper range",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "Number of students") +
  theme_ipsum(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11))




The second goal is about making chart production faster and more consistent. In other words, hrbrthemes is designed for a production-style workflow, where charts need to look clean, readable, and publication-ready with less manual formatting. See the vignette for more details.
The second goal of hrbrthemes is to make chart production faster and more consistent. Instead of manually adjusting every small design detail, theme_ipsum() gives the plot cleaner typography, better spacing, and a more report-ready layout. This is useful when the same visual style needs to be reused across many charts.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 linewidth = 0.35,
                 alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores",
       subtitle = "A cleaner report-style histogram using hrbrthemes",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "Number of students") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 16,
              base_size = 14,
              plot_title_size = 20,
              subtitle_size = 12,
              grid = "Y") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#555555"))










Tip



What can we learn from this code?
This code shows how hrbrthemes helps create a more polished chart with less manual styling.

theme_ipsum() changes the overall typography and spacing of the plot.
base_size = 14 makes the chart text easier to read.
plot_title_size = 20 makes the title more prominent.
grid = "Y" keeps only the horizontal grid lines, so the chart looks cleaner.
The muted teal bar colour gives the chart a more consistent visual identity.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
2.5 Beyond Single Grap
A single chart is useful, but it rarely tells the whole story. For example, one graph may show the distribution of Maths scores, while another may show how Maths and English scores move together.
To make the analysis easier to compare, we can place several ggplot2 graphs into one combined figure. In this section, I will first create three separate plots, then use a ggplot2 extension to arrange them into one visual layout.

Maths graphEnglish graphRelationship graphR code


























`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'












p1 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#2A6F6F",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

p2 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "#1F1F1F",
                 fill = "#8AA6A3",
                 linewidth = 0.35) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p3 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = MATHS,
                 y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(color = "#1F1F1F") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = "#2A6F6F") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores")





2.5.1 Creating Composite Graphics: pathwork methods
There are several ggplot2 extension packages that can combine multiple graphs into one composite figure. For example, `gridExtra` uses `grid.arrange()`, while `cowplot` uses `plot_grid()`.

In this section, I will use another extension package called `patchwork`. It is designed specifically for combining separate ggplot2 plots into one layout, and its syntax is very easy to read.

The basic idea is simple:

`+` places plots side by side in a two-column layout.
`/` stacks plots vertically in a two-row layout.
`()` groups plots together before combining them with other plots.

This makes `patchwork` useful when we want to build a clear visual story using several related charts.


2.5.2 Combining two ggplot2 graphs
Figure in the tabset below shows a composite of two histograms created using patchwork. Note how simple the syntax used to create the plot!

GraphR code














p1 + p2 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Comparing Maths and English Score Distributions",
    subtitle = "Two histograms arranged side by side using patchwork"
  )






2.5.3 Combining three ggplot2 graphs
Now we can combine three ggplot2 graphs into a more useful layout.
In this example, the two histograms are stacked on the left, while the scatter plot is placed on the right. This layout works better than showing three separate charts because we can compare the Maths and English score distributions first, then look at their relationship in the larger plot.

ViewCode




`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'












((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Maths and English Scores: Distribution and Relationship",
    subtitle = "Histograms show the score distributions, while the scatter plot shows how the two subjects are related."
  )




To learn more about, refer to Plot Assembly.


2.5.4 Creating a composite figure with tag
When a composite figure contains several plots, it can become hard to refer to each subplot clearly in the explanation. patchwork solves this by adding automatic tags such as A, B, and C.
This is useful in reports because we can write things like “Plot A shows the Maths distribution” or “Plot C shows the relationship between Maths and English scores” without confusing the reader.

ViewCode




`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'












((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Maths and English Scores: Distribution and Relationship",
    subtitle = "Tagged subplots make the combined figure easier to discuss.",
    tag_levels = "A"
  )






2.5.5 Creating figure with insert
Sometimes we do not want every plot to take equal space in a composite figure. A smaller chart can be inserted into a larger chart to provide extra context without breaking the main visual flow.
In this example, the scatter plot remains the main graph because it shows the relationship between Maths and English scores. The English score histogram is inserted inside the scatter plot, so the viewer can also see the distribution of English scores without moving to a separate figure.

View




`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'










p3 +
  inset_element(
    p2,
    left = 0.05,
    bottom = 0.68,
    right = 0.52,
    top = 0.98
  ) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "English Scores versus Maths Scores",
    subtitle = "The inserted histogram gives extra context about the English score distribution."
  )






2.5.6 Creating a composite figure by using patchwork and ggtheme
This example combines two ideas from earlier sections: patchwork for layout and ggthemes for visual styling.
Instead of only placing the three plots together, we also apply a consistent theme across the whole composite figure. This makes the final output look more unified, as if the charts belong to the same report instead of being three separate graphics pasted together.

ViewCode




`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'












((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Maths and English Scores: A Combined View",
    subtitle = "Patchwork controls the layout, while ggthemes gives the full figure a consistent visual style."
  ) &
  theme_economist() &
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b - Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated graphics can make a data story easier to follow because the viewer can see how values change over time instead of comparing many separate static charts. In this exercise, we will use gganimate and plotly to create animated statistical graphics in R.
At the same time, we will also practise basic data preparation skills, including importing Excel data, converting data types, and reshaping variables with modern dplyr syntax.
By the end of this exercise, we will be able to:

explain how animation works in statistical graphics,
prepare population data for animated visualisation,
create static and animated bubble plots,
use gganimate to animate ggplot2 graphics,
use plotly to create interactive animated charts.



An animated chart does not really “move” by itself. It is created from many individual plots, where each plot represents one moment in time. When these plots are shown quickly in sequence, the viewer sees motion.
This is similar to a flipbook: each page is static, but the sequence creates the feeling of movement. In data visualisation, each frame usually represents a year, a category, or a time period.






TipSmall note



Animation is useful when time is part of the story. If the data does not change meaningfully across time, a static chart may be clearer and faster to read.





Before creating animated charts, it is useful to understand a few basic terms.
A frame is one still image inside the animation. For example, in a population animation, each year can become one frame.
Animation attributes control how the animation behaves. These include the speed of movement, how smoothly one frame changes into another, and whether the animation starts from the beginning or continues from the current frame.






WarningWarning



Animated graphics should not be used only because they look interesting. If the movement does not help the reader understand the data, the animation becomes decoration rather than analysis.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b - Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
4.2 Getting Started
Before building the charts, we need to load the required R packages.

4.2.1 Loading the R packages
The packages used in this exercise are:

readxl for importing Excel files,
gganimate for creating animated ggplot2 graphics,
gifski for rendering animations as GIFs,
gapminder for using the country_colors palette,
plotly for interactive animated charts,
tidyverse for data import, wrangling, and static graphics.







WarningWarning



gganimate may take longer to render than normal ggplot2 charts because it needs to generate many frames. If the render feels slow, this is normal.




4.2.2 Importing the data
In this exercise, we use the Data worksheet from the GlobalPopulation.xls workbook.
Place GlobalPopulation.xls in the same folder as this file:
Hands-on_Ex03b/
  Hands-on_Ex03b.qmd
  GlobalPopulation.xls

ViewCode




Rows: 6,204
Columns: 6
$ Country    <fct> "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan",…
$ Year       <int> 1996, 1998, 2000, 2002, 2004, 2006, 2008, 2010, 2012, 2014,…
$ Young      <dbl> 83.6, 84.1, 84.6, 85.1, 84.5, 84.3, 84.1, 83.7, 82.9, 82.1,…
$ Old        <dbl> 4.5, 4.5, 4.5, 4.5, 4.5, 4.6, 4.6, 4.6, 4.6, 4.7, 4.7, 4.7,…
$ Population <dbl> 21559.9, 22912.8, 23898.2, 25268.4, 28513.7, 31057.0, 32738…
$ Continent  <fct> Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia,…





globalPop <- read_xls("GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(col), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

country_palette <- setNames(
  rep(country_colors, length.out = nlevels(globalPop$Country)),
  levels(globalPop$Country)
)

glimpse(globalPop)




The code above imports the Excel worksheet and cleans the key variable types. Country and Continent are converted into factor variables, while Year is converted into an integer. This makes the data easier to use for grouping, colouring, and animation.






TipWhat can we learn?



The original tutorial shows older dplyr functions such as mutate_each_() and mutate_at(). In this version, across() is used because it is the modern and recommended way to apply the same transformation to multiple columns.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b - Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
section: 4.2.3 A quick static summary before animation
text:
4.2.3 A quick static summary before animation
Before creating an animation, it is useful to inspect the overall pattern with a simple static chart. This small summary compares the average share of young and old population by continent.
This is not the main chart of the chapter, but it helps us understand what the animation will later show: population structure differs across continents.

ViewCode














continent_summary <- globalPop %>%
  group_by(Continent) %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Young = mean(Young, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Old = mean(Old, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Avg_Young, Avg_Old),
    names_to = "Age_Group",
    values_to = "Average_Percentage"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Age_Group = recode(
      Age_Group,
      "Avg_Young" = "Young population",
      "Avg_Old" = "Old population"
    )
  )

ggplot(continent_summary,
       aes(x = Continent,
           y = Average_Percentage,
           fill = Age_Group)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge",
           color = my_dark,
           linewidth = 0.35,
           alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(my_teal, my_soft_teal)) +
  labs(title = "Average Young and Old Population by Continent",
       subtitle = "A quick static view before creating the animation",
       x = "Continent",
       y = "Average percentage",
       fill = "Age group") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "top")










TipWhat can we learn?



This chart is a small checkpoint. Before animating the data, we first check whether the key variables have meaningful differences across continents.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b - Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
4.3 Animated Data Visualisation: gganimate methods
gganimate extends ggplot2 by adding animation grammar. Instead of only describing the static appearance of a chart, we can also describe how the chart changes across time.
Important animation components include:

transition_*() defines how the animation moves across states or time.
view_*() controls how the plot window changes during the animation.
shadow_*() shows data from earlier or later frames.
enter_*() and exit_*() control how objects appear or disappear.
ease_aes() controls how smoothly values move between frames.


4.3.1 Building a static population bubble plot
Before creating an animated chart, we first build a static bubble plot. Each point represents a country. The x-axis shows the percentage of old population, the y-axis shows the percentage of young population, and bubble size represents total population.

ViewCode














ggplot(globalPop,
       aes(x = Old,
           y = Young,
           size = Population,
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors,
                      na.value = my_teal) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Static Population Bubble Plot",
       subtitle = "Each bubble represents a country",
       x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young",
       size = "Population") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())




This static version is useful because it checks whether the base chart works before animation is added. If the static chart is unclear, the animated version will usually be even harder to understand.


4.3.2 Building the animated bubble plot
Now we add animation by using transition_time(Year). This tells gganimate to create frames according to the Year variable.
The animation shows how the relationship between old population share, young population share, and population size changes over time.

ViewCode






animated_bubble <- ggplot(globalPop,
                          aes(x = Old,
                              y = Young,
                              size = Population,
                              colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors,
                      na.value = my_teal) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Population structure changes over time",
       x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young",
       size = "Population") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = my_dark),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = my_teal),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

animate(animated_bubble,
        nframes = 80,
        fps = 10,
        width = 800,
        height = 500,
        renderer = gifski_renderer())










TipWhat can we learn?



transition_time(Year) creates the time-based animation, while ease_aes("linear") makes the movement between years steady. The title uses {frame_time}, so the displayed year updates automatically.








WarningWarning



If this chunk takes time to render, do not panic. Animated plots are heavier than normal static plots because many frames are generated before the final GIF appears.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b - Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
4.4 Animated Data Visualisation: plotly
plotly can also create animated bubble plots. The difference is that plotly animations are interactive. Readers can play, pause, drag the slider, hover over points, and zoom into the chart.
There are two ways to create animated plotly graphics in this section:

use ggplotly() to convert a ggplot object,
use plot_ly() to build the animated chart directly.


4.4.1 Building an animated bubble plot: ggplotly() method
The first plotly method starts with a ggplot object. The frame = Year aesthetic tells plotly that the chart should animate by year.

ViewCode











gg <- ggplot(globalPop,
             aes(x = Old,
                 y = Young,
                 size = Population,
                 colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors,
                      na.value = my_teal) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young",
       title = "Animated Bubble Plot with ggplotly") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

ggplotly(gg)




The chart now includes a play button and a time slider. This makes the animation easier to control compared with a fixed GIF.
However, show.legend = FALSE may not always remove the legend after conversion to plotly. To handle this more reliably, we can use theme(legend.position = "none").

ViewCode











gg_clean <- ggplot(globalPop,
                   aes(x = Old,
                       y = Young,
                       size = Population,
                       colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors,
                      na.value = my_teal) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young",
       title = "Animated Bubble Plot without Legend") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "none")

ggplotly(gg_clean)










TipWhat can we learn?



ggplotly() is useful when we already know how to build the chart with ggplot2. We create the ggplot first, then convert it into an interactive animated chart.




4.4.2 Building an animated bubble plot: plot_ly() method
The second method uses plot_ly() directly. This gives more direct control over plotly-specific features such as frame, hover text, marker size, and legend display.

ViewCode











bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(
    x = ~Old,
    y = ~Young,
    size = ~Population,
    color = ~Continent,
    sizes = c(2, 100),
    frame = ~Year,
    text = ~paste0(
      "Country: ", Country,
      "<br>Continent: ", Continent,
      "<br>Year: ", Year,
      "<br>% Aged: ", Old,
      "<br>% Young: ", Young,
      "<br>Population: ", comma(Population)
    ),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    type = "scatter",
    mode = "markers",
    marker = list(
      opacity = 0.75,
      line = list(color = my_dark, width = 0.5)
    )
  ) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Animated Population Bubble Plot with plot_ly",
    xaxis = list(title = "% Aged"),
    yaxis = list(title = "% Young"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

bp




This version is built directly with plotly syntax. The hover text is more detailed, so readers can inspect each country, continent, year, age structure, and population value.






TipWhat can we learn?



plot_ly() is more direct than ggplotly(). It is useful when the final output is intended to be an interactive plotly chart from the start.








WarningWarning



Because the data contains many countries and many years, the plotly animation may take some time to load in the browser. This is normal for animated interactive charts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b - Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
4.5 Reference
This exercise introduced animated statistical graphics using two main approaches.
gganimate is useful when we want to keep the ggplot2 workflow and produce a smooth animated GIF. It works well for presentation-style storytelling.
plotly is useful when we want the viewer to control the animation with play buttons, sliders, zooming, and hover labels. It works better for web-based exploration.
Useful references:

gganimate documentation for animation grammar and transitions,
plotly documentation for interactive animated charts,
readxl documentation for importing Excel worksheets,
